text.title: Migration Signals and Paths: Monolith to Microservices
text.summary: Observable warning signs that a monolith has grown past its optimal size; the recommended migration path Monolith → Modular Monolith → Selective Microservices; and the Strangler Fig pattern for incremental extraction.

section.title: Warning Signs the Monolith Has Outgrown Itself

rule.statement: Do not migrate from monolith until at least two of the following signals are sustained for 60+ days: consistently blocked deployments, measurably different scaling profiles per module, independent team release cadences blocked by shared codebase, or build+test time exceeding 30 minutes.
rule.rationale: Premature decomposition is the most common distributed systems mistake. Teams extract services before understanding domain boundaries, creating distributed monoliths — all the complexity of microservices with none of the independence. Wait for real signals, not hypothetical future need.

list.item: Deployment bottleneck: multiple teams block each other's releases because all code ships together
Scaling mismatch: the order-processing module needs 10x the capacity of the user-profile module, but they scale as one unit
Build time >30 min: a full build+test cycle blocks fast iteration
Module boundaries violated: circular dependencies in the static analysis report that cannot be resolved
Independent team ownership: two teams own different domains but share the same deployment pipeline and are constantly in each other's code
Technology mismatch: one domain genuinely requires a different runtime (Python ML module in a Go monolith)

rule.statement: The correct migration path is Monolith → Modular Monolith → extract one service at a time. Never skip the modular monolith step — it proves the module boundaries and data isolation before adding network hops.
rule.rationale: Extracting a module to a service that is not already isolated in a modular monolith requires solving data isolation, API design, and distributed transactions simultaneously. Modular monolith first solves these one at a time in a much lower-risk context.

list.item: Step 1: Apply Modular Monolith boundaries — enforce via import-linter or ArchUnit in CI
Step 2: Isolate data — each module uses its own schema; no direct cross-module table reads
Step 3: Replace cross-module direct calls with explicit events or service interfaces (prepare for async)
Step 4: Extract the highest-signal module first (highest traffic or fastest-changing team)
Step 5: Use Strangler Fig — the new service handles new requests; the monolith handles existing ones; gradually migrate

rule.statement: Extract functionality incrementally using the Strangler Fig pattern: place a routing facade in front of the monolith, route specific requests to the new service, and shrink the monolith progressively. Never rewrite the entire monolith at once.
rule.rationale: Big-bang rewrites fail at a high rate because the new system must replicate all behavior of the old system while the old system continues to evolve. Strangler Fig keeps the old system running and moves risk to the routing layer, which can be tested independently.

list.item: Phase 1: Introduce routing facade (nginx, API gateway, or feature flag) in front of monolith
Phase 2: Build new service for one bounded context; deploy behind the facade
Phase 3: Route traffic for that context to the new service (canary first)
Phase 4: Remove the corresponding code from the monolith once the new service is stable
Phase 5: Repeat for next bounded context
